prompt: Most of the code you write runs in a browser tab.
Your current project has fewer than ten engineers.
You own both the visual design decisions and the component code.
React is your default choice for building UI.
Accessibility audits are part of your definition of done.
Utility-first CSS beats writing custom class hierarchies every time.
Storybook is open in a browser tab most of your working day.
Your code ships to a mobile app store, not a web URL.
Your users are consumers, not other developers.
React Native or Flutter is your primary client-side tool.
You also own the API and database that power your client app.
Design tokens are a first-class concern in your component architecture.
Your component library lives in Storybook with a written contribution guide.
CSS specificity is something you can explain to a junior in five minutes.
You write prop-table documentation before merging a new component.
Core Web Vitals failures block a deploy in your team.
Bundle analysis and tree-shaking are regular parts of your review checklist.
Partial hydration beats shipping JavaScript you do not need.
Profiling a page in DevTools is something you do most weeks.
Expo or the React Native CLI is the starting point for new projects.
App store review cycles are a real constraint on your release cadence.
Platform-specific APIs like push notifications and biometrics are your domain.
Pixel-perfect fidelity on both iOS and Android matters to you.
A single feature spans your database schema, API, and UI — all yours.
GraphQL or a typed REST client connects your frontend and backend layers.
API contract design is something you treat as a craft, not an afterthought.
Shipping a feature end-to-end solo is your normal, not an exception.
Your pull requests always include screenshots of before and after states.
The consumers of your components are other developers on your team.
A component that looks right but fails an a11y audit is not done.
Server-side rendering and edge caching are tools you reach for by default.
Your optimisations target millions of page views, not thousands.
WebAssembly or WebGPU has come up in a real architectural conversation of yours.
You write device-specific test plans before submitting to an app store.
You have written or patched a native module to bridge a gap in a cross-platform SDK.
A native feel on each platform beats writing it once and shipping everywhere.
The product you build serves a lot more than a handful of internal users.
End users experience the product you build, not just the team shipping it.
Shipping a rough but working feature beats a perfect one that takes twice as long.

axis: OWNERSHIP — infra lifecycle vs feature delivery
prompt: Your work ships infrastructure and tooling, not features for end users.

axis: MEDIUM/SURFACE — cloud vs bare-metal vs local
prompt: Most of what you run lives in a cloud provider, not on bare metal.

axis: WORKFLOW — operational/on-call rhythms vs project delivery
prompt: Keeping systems healthy at 2am is a real part of your job.
Terraform or Pulumi is how your team provisions every cloud resource.
You manage clusters or fleets big enough that manual changes are forbidden.

axis: PHILOSOPHY — golden paths vs team freedom
prompt: Giving teams a golden path matters more to you than letting them choose their own stack.

axis: AUDIENCE — internal platform consumers vs service reliability
prompt: Other engineering teams are the primary consumers of what you build.

axis: AUDIENCE — developers as primary users of your output
prompt: Other developers are the end users of everything you ship.

axis: CRAFT/RIGOUR — security posture and threat awareness
prompt: Threat modelling is a recurring activity on your team, not a one-off audit.

axis: TOOLING — CLI/build tools vs security scanning tools
prompt: Build speed and local developer feedback loops are where you spend your energy.

axis: PHILOSOPHY — shift-left security vs DX and productivity
prompt: Security catches in the IDE or PR pipeline beat anything found in production.
Kubernetes is the substrate everything else runs on in your world.
Your platform spans more than one cloud provider by design.

axis: PROCESS/ARTIFACTS — IaC modules, golden paths, developer portals
prompt: You own the Terraform modules or Helm charts that other teams fork and extend.

axis: DEPTH — platform engineering specialist vs generalist devops
prompt: Platform engineering is your identity, not just one part of a broader devops role.

axis: WORKFLOW — on-call ownership and pager culture
prompt: Pager rotations and incident response windows shape how your week is structured.
Error budgets and SLO burn-rate alerts are part of your standard toolkit.
Prometheus, Grafana, and OpenTelemetry are daily-use tools, not occasional references.

axis: CRAFT/RIGOUR — chaos engineering and incident retrospectives
prompt: Controlled failure injection is a legitimate way to harden your systems.

axis: OWNERSHIP — supply chain and pipeline security
prompt: The security of the build and release pipeline is your primary concern.

axis: TOOLING — SAST, DAST, and vulnerability scanning
prompt: Static analysis and dependency scanning tools run on every pull request.

axis: PHILOSOPHY — security as sprint-planning participant vs post-launch gate
prompt: Your team treats security reviews as part of sprint planning, not a pre-launch checkbox.

axis: CRAFT/RIGOUR — threat modelling and offensive security
prompt: You have run a threat model or a pen test exercise in the last six months.

axis: AUDIENCE — other engineers as first-class users
prompt: Your success metric is how fast other engineers can ship, not what they ship.

axis: TOOLING — compilers, build systems, CLI tools
prompt: Compilers, linkers, or build graphs are where you spend real engineering time.

axis: PHILOSOPHY — DX measured in friction removed
prompt: Reducing time-to-first-green-build is a legitimate product goal for your team.

axis: PROCESS/ARTIFACTS — open-source tooling and plugin ecosystems
prompt: Open-source is the default distribution model for what your team produces.

axis: SCALE — number of teams served by the platform
prompt: Your internal developer platform serves ten or more engineering teams simultaneously.

axis: MEDIUM/SURFACE — edge and hybrid cloud patterns
prompt: Edge compute or hybrid-cloud routing is something your platform already handles.
Provisioning new clusters or cloud accounts is a self-service flow, not a ticket.

axis: SCALE — service count and traffic volume
prompt: Your observability stack covers hundreds of services across multiple regions.
Post-mortems at your company produce action items that actually get closed.
Reliability is a feature your team owns from day one, not a handoff after launch.

axis: DEPTH — specialist appsec vs broad DevSecOps generalist
prompt: Application security is your deep specialty, not a shared responsibility you rotate through.

axis: WORKFLOW — async security review cadence vs embedded sprint presence
prompt: Your security work runs alongside active sprints, not as a separate gate process.
A vulnerability without a clear owner is your problem until it has one.

axis: DEPTH — specialist tooling author vs generalist developer productivity
prompt: You are the person who builds the tools, not just the person who configures them.

axis: CRAFT/RIGOUR — language-level and runtime performance concerns
prompt: Startup latency and binary size are real constraints in the tools you write.
A well-crafted CLI feels as good to use as a well-designed API.
Your day-to-day is closer to data pipelines than to product UIs.
You own the model or the pipeline — not just the API call that wraps it.
Model quality and training dynamics interest you more than business dashboards.
PyTorch or TensorFlow lives in your environment, not just your reading list.
Experiment tracking and eval harnesses are non-negotiable parts of your workflow.
Open-weights fine-tuning beats hitting a hosted API for your use cases.
You've wrestled with serving a model under real production traffic, not just a demo.
Pipelines and data movement occupy more of your week than SQL queries do.
Your primary consumers are other engineering systems, not business stakeholders.
Spark, Kafka, or Flink are closer to home than dbt or Tableau.
Pipeline reliability and data freshness SLAs keep you up, not metric definitions.
A thorough eval harness is table stakes before any model hits production.
Model cards and reproducible training runs are part of your standard deliverables.
Compute budget and GPU utilisation heavily shape your model architectural decisions.
You've tuned serving latency and throughput for models beyond a single GPU instance.
LangChain, LlamaIndex, or a similar orchestration layer is central to your builds.
Iterating on prompts and retrieval strategies takes more time than writing application code.
Your end product is a user-facing feature, not a research artifact or internal tool.
Grounding, citation accuracy, and hallucination rate are metrics you actively track.
You are on-call for data pipelines the same way a backend engineer is on-call for services.
CDC, schema registries, or event-streaming platforms are in your production stack.
Streaming beats batch for most of the problems you care about solving.
Your pipelines move petabytes or handle millions of events per second, not just gigabytes.
A well-structured dbt model is the artefact you take the most pride in shipping.
Metric definitions and data contracts matter more to you than dashboard prettiness.
Most of your work ends up as a dashboard or a number a non-technical stakeholder reads.
SQL is your primary language — Python is a tool you reach for, not your home base.
You own the full lifecycle — training, packaging, and deploying — not just a training script.
Notebooks are a starting point — your real work lives in a proper codebase and CI.
Architecture papers and training techniques excite you as much as a good benchmark score.
You care more about what the model can do for users than how it was trained.
Shipping a new AI feature means wiring context, tools, and retrieval — not touching model weights.
Your deliverable is a working product integration, not a research notebook or a trained model.
Data quality checks and schema validation are built into your pipelines from day one.
You treat data infrastructure the way platform teams treat compute — always on, always reliable.
Reproducible, well-documented pipelines are your craft — messy notebooks make you uncomfortable.
Your analytical stack includes a BI layer that non-engineers actually open and trust.
A single reliable metric definition beats ten dashboard tabs of similar-looking numbers.
Translating business questions into clean, testable models is the part of the job you enjoy most.
Your work targets a physical device or interactive experience, not a web service.
Your daily toolchain is built around native SDKs, game engines, or embedded runtimes.
You personally write more code than you review, delegate, or pitch.
Your primary output runs on a screen and is meant to be played.
A game engine — Unity, Unreal, or Godot — is your primary development environment.
Your end users are players or gamers, not operators, OEM customers, or field engineers.
Gameplay feel and player delight matter more to you than deterministic timing budgets.
Growing and unblocking a team of engineers is the core of your role.
You operate inside an existing organisation rather than building one from scratch.
OKRs, performance reviews, and headcount planning are recurring deliverables in your role.
Your biggest accountability is to the people on your team, not to investors or a board.
You have shipped at least one game to a public store or platform.
Crunch windows and milestone reviews are a familiar part of your release cycle.
You run structured playtesting sessions before declaring a feature complete.
Graphics, physics, or engine internals draw your curiosity more than game design.
Your code runs on a microcontroller or SoC with no general-purpose operating system.
Hardware bring-up and board-level debugging are routine parts of your sprint.
Certification standards or safety constraints — MISRA, ISO 26262, IEC 62443 — shape your code reviews.
Memory footprint and interrupt latency are metrics you track as obsessively as throughput.
Your most important stakeholder conversations are with engineers on your team, not executives.
Your week contains more one-on-ones and team rituals than design or architecture sessions.
You track team health metrics and delivery velocity the way others track code coverage.
Servant leadership and psychological safety are concepts you reference in your own words.
Investors and potential customers are audiences you prepare for more than your own engineers.
Fundraising, investor updates, and runway planning are recurring items on your calendar.
A pitch deck you authored directly closed a funding round or a major customer.
Setting the technical direction of the whole company falls on your shoulders.
Playable build demos and vertical-slice reviews are standard checkpoints in your project.
A profiler is always open in a second window when you are iterating on a scene.
Fun is an engineering constraint you hold as seriously as frame rate.
Firmware binaries, flashing scripts, and hardware test rigs are standard deliverables in your project.
GCC, Clang, or a vendor toolchain — never a browser-based IDE — is where you spend your day.
Bare metal feels like home; an OS is a luxury you design around, not a default.
Engineering all-hands decks, growth memos, and hiring rubrics are artefacts you own.
Linear, Notion, or a roadmap tool gets more of your attention than a code editor.
Your team shipping without you is the sign of your best work.
Pitch decks, investor updates, and growth memos are deliverables you produce yourself.
Your most-used tools include a spreadsheet model, a CRM, and a slide deck — not a linter.
Debugging production at 2 am and a board presentation at 9 am — same day.
